summary:
The Topper Site is an archaeological site on the Savannah River in Allendale County, South Carolina, USA, excavated primarily by Albert Goodyear of the University of South Carolina since the 1980s. The site is known for a well-documented Clovis occupation as well as highly controversial earlier deposits that Goodyear has interpreted as containing pre-Clovis artifacts potentially dating to 50,000 BCE or earlier.

For the period 10,000–8,000 BCE, the Topper Site contains a Clovis-era and post-Clovis occupational sequence documenting Paleo-Indian hunters in the Piedmont region of the southeastern United States. This culturally unambiguous horizon provides a baseline against which the more controversial earlier deposits are compared. The Clovis and Dalton-age (post-Clovis) occupations at Topper fit within the established pattern of Paleo-Indian habitation of the southeastern US, with populations hunting game, fishing in the Savannah River, and exploiting the diverse resources of the Piedmont landscape. The site's importance lies both in its well-documented Clovis assemblage and in the ongoing scientific debate about the possible pre-Clovis deposits in its deeper layers, which bear on fundamental questions about the timing of the first human entry into North America.
